_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Miami County, OH. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Miami County was formed in 1807 from Montgomery County, with Troy as county seat. Located on the Great Miami River and the Miami & Erie Canal corridor, Miami County holds continuous records from formation with no significant courthouse losses.

previous_courthouses.fate: Outgrown and replaced. Records transferred intact.

previous_courthouses.location: Troy, frame courthouse on Main Street
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced with a brick building. Records survived.

previous_courthouses.location: Troy, brick courthouse on Main Street
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the 1888 Second Empire-style building. Records survived transfer.

record_losses.description: Miami County has experienced no courthouse fire and no significant record loss. The Great Miami River flooded periodically, but the courthouse location on higher ground in Troy protected records. Records run continuously from the 1807 formation. For ancestors present before 1807, researchers should check Montgomery County records, as Miami County was carved from Montgomery.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Miami County's complete record survival means that gaps in {ancestor_name}'s paper trail are circumstantial. The county's position on the Miami & Erie Canal route means canal-related records — toll records, canal company papers, and land condemnation records for the canal right-of-way — supplement standard courthouse records.
record_losses.source: History of Miami County, Ohio. Chicago: W.H. Beers & Co., 1880.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county formation. Includes canal land records (easements, condemnation) from the Miami & Erie Canal era.
deeds.gaps: No known gaps. Pre-1807 transactions in this territory appear in Montgomery County deed books.
deeds.repository: Miami County Recorder, Troy; Ohio History Connection microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com OH land records

wills_estates.completeness: Good — probate records from county formation. Estate inventories document farming families and the small-town merchant class.
wills_estates.gaps: Early loose papers fragile but generally legible.
wills_estates.repository: Miami County Probate Court, Troy; Ohio History Connection

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage records from county formation
marriages.repository: Miami County Probate Court; Ohio History Connection microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good — Court of Common Pleas minutes from formation
court_minutes.gaps: Justice of the peace records incomplete for early decades.
court_minutes.repository: Miami County Clerk of Courts; Ohio History Connection

vital_records.completeness: Ohio statewide registration began 1908. Some probate court birth/death records from the 1860s-1907 under earlier Ohio statutes.
vital_records.repository: Miami County Probate Court; Ohio Department of Health; Troy-Miami County Public Library

substitute_sources.description: The Miami & Erie Canal (built 1825-1845) ran through Miami County along the Great Miami River, with locks and basin facilities near Troy and Piqua. Canal company records, toll books, and land condemnation records document the workers, operators, and landowners affected by the canal.
substitute_sources.repository: Ohio History Connection (Board of Public Works records); Miami County Historical and Genealogical Society; National Archives regional collections
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Canal construction records document Irish and German laborers who built the canal and often stayed in the communities where they worked. Toll records track commerce and the names of boat operators. Land condemnation records identify property owners along the canal right-of-way.
substitute_sources.source: Gieck, Jack. A Photo Album of Ohio's Canal Era, 1825-1913. Kent, OH: Kent State University Press, 1988.

substitute_sources.source_type: German Baptist Brethren and German Reformed church records
substitute_sources.description: Miami County had significant German settlement, with Brethren congregations and German Reformed churches maintaining records in German. These records are particularly valuable for the Covington, Pleasant Hill, and western Miami County communities.
substitute_sources.repository: Brethren Historical Library and Archives, Elgin, IL; Evangelical and Reformed Historical Society, Lancaster, PA; individual congregations
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: German-language church records document families who migrated from Pennsylvania and Virginia. Baptismal records list godparents, revealing kinship networks. Membership transfers track migration routes.
substitute_sources.source: Durnbaugh, Donald F. Fruit of the Vine: A History of the Brethren, 1708-1995. Elgin, IL: Brethren Press, 1997.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1810–1940. Agricultural schedules (1850–1880) document farming families. Manufacturing schedules capture Troy's and Piqua's small-scale industries.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families. Agricultural schedules document the transition from subsistence to commercial farming along the canal corridor.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Miami County, OH.

narrative_hooks: Miami County's records run from 1807 without a break — the courthouse survived every flood that hit Troy, and if {ancestor_name} bought land, married, or died here, the record almost certainly survives.

The Miami & Erie Canal ran right through {ancestor_name}'s county — and the canal records may document {pronoun_object} as a landowner whose property was condemned for the right-of-way, a laborer who dug the ditch, or a boatman who hauled goods on the water.
Before 1807, Miami County did not exist — {ancestor_name}'s earliest records are in Montgomery County, and the first chapter of the family's story in this territory is filed in the Dayton courthouse.

If {ancestor_name} was German Baptist Brethren in Miami County, the church records at the Elgin archives track the migration chain from Pennsylvania through Virginia to the Ohio frontier — the congregation moved as a community, and the records followed.
source_references: History of Miami County, Ohio. Chicago: W.H. Beers & Co., 1880.
Gieck, Jack. A Photo Album of Ohio's Canal Era, 1825-1913. Kent, OH: Kent State University Press, 1988.
Durnbaugh, Donald F. Fruit of the Vine: A History of the Brethren, 1708-1995. Elgin, IL: Brethren Press, 1997.
Ohio History Connection. County Records Guide: Miami County.
